coredata.dc:title: Super-stable carbon quantum dots nanofluid for efficient solar-thermal conversion
coredata.dc:creator: Seifikar, Fatemeh
Azizian, Saeid
coredata.dc:description: Environmental-friendly carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanofluid was synthesized using microwave heating of polyethyleneglycol 200 (PEG 200). PEG acts as a carbon source, base fluid and dispersant simultaneously. The synthesized CQDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS) and fluorescence spectroscopy. The synthesized CQDs with average diameter of 6.5 nm were solvated with PEG molecules, and consequently, the nanofluid with an excellent long-term stability was obtained. The synthesized CQDs nanofluid was used for photo-thermal conversion. The infrared thermographs showed that the surface temperature of nanofluid reaches to >60 °C after irradiation using LED lamp with 0.8 Sun irradiance for 100 min. The synthesized nanofluid was used at several consecutive heating/cooling cycles without losing its efficiency in photo-thermal conversion. Total absorbed energy, stored energy ratio, photo-thermal conversion efficiency and rate constant of heat dissipation were calculated and discussed in details. Because of stability at high temperatures (up to 200 °C), preservation of photo-thermal efficiency at consecutive heating/cooling cycles and longtime durability, the synthesized CQDs nanofluid can be considered as a super-stable nanofluid. Additionally, the performance of the CQDs nanofluid in solar energy harvesting and its application for water heating in a continuous flow system was examined. All results confirm that the synthesized CQDs nanofluid has many advantages including low cost and simplicity of preparation procedure, no need to use dispersant, long-life stability, very high thermal-stability and high photo-thermal conversion efficiency. Therefore, CQDs nanofluid has high potential to be utilized for practical solar energy harvesting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solar energy harvesting
Microwave irradiation
Polyethylene glycol 200
Carbon quantum dots
Warming of water
Nanofluid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reusability of the synthesized CQDs nanofluid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic of the used steps for synthesis of the carbon nanofluid.
The main steps of the photo-thermal conversion experiments.
(a–c) The TEM images of the CQDs nanofluid with different magnifications and (d) the particle size distribution histogram.

(a) DLS histogram of the hydrodynamic particle size (dH ) distribution of the CQDs nanofluid, diluted with PEG 200 (10 times). (b) Schematic image of the dispersed CQDs in PEG and their hydrodynamic diameter. (c) The optical path of laser light in the diluted CQDs nanofluid (i) and in deionized water (ii).

(a) and (b) The fluorescence and the normalized fluorescence emission spectra of the synthesized CQDs nanofluid (100 times diluted with PEG) after excitation at various wavelengths. (c) Mechanism of absorption/emission of light and heat generation by CQDs with different sizes.
Correlated plot of the particle size distribution of CQDs and the relative fluorescence emission intensity at different wavelengths.

(a) Time-dependent temperature profiles at different depths and (b) the infrared thermographs from different views of the pristine fluids and the synthesized carbon nanofluids after 100 min of light irradiation.
Time-dependent temperature profiles of the synthesized CQDs nanofluid for six-consecutive heating/cooling cycles.

(a) The calculated SER values versus time for the synthesized CQDs nanofluid (P10) at different heights. (b) The photo-thermal conversion efficiency (η) of the CQDs nanofluid vs. temperature at bulk and at three-different heights (H1, H2, and H3).

vs. time for cooling step at three-different heights (H1, H2, and H3).
(a) and (b) variation of nanofluids temperature vs. time under sun-light irradiation at 0.8 and 10 Sun, respectively.
(a) The optical image and (b) infrared thermographs of the carbon nanofluid after 30 min of sun-light irradiation.
A simple system of solar-thermal conversion for heating of a continuous flow of water.
Maximum and minimum fluid temperatures and the calculated absorbed energy.
A comparison between some properties of the synthesized carbon nanofluid and others one.

row.entry: FeCl3·6H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, deionized water, ammonium hydroxide, PEG, ethanol, TEOS, nitrogen gas
Chemical co-precipitation method and modified Stöber method
Graphite powder, NaNO3, KMnO4, H2O2, deionized water, ammonia water, L-AA
FeCl2·4H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, nitrogen, NH3, H2O, ethanol, TiF4
Commercial carbon black powder, H2O2, distilled water
Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, ethanol, furfuryl alcohol, SBA-15, N2 gas, NaOH, deionized water, EG

head.ce:title: Super-stable carbon quantum dots nanofluid for efficient solar-thermal conversion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Environmental-friendly carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanofluid was synthesized using microwave heating of polyethyleneglycol 200 (PEG 200). PEG acts as a carbon source, base fluid and dispersant simultaneously. The synthesized CQDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), dynamic light scattering (DLS) and fluorescence spectroscopy. The synthesized CQDs with average diameter of 6.5 nm were solvated with PEG molecules, and consequently, the nanofluid with an excellent long-term stability was obtained. The synthesized CQDs nanofluid was used for photo-thermal conversion. The infrared thermographs showed that the surface temperature of nanofluid reaches to >60 °C after irradiation using LED lamp with 0.8 Sun irradiance for 100 min. The synthesized nanofluid was used at several consecutive heating/cooling cycles without losing its efficiency in photo-thermal conversion. Total absorbed energy, stored energy ratio, photo-thermal conversion efficiency and rate constant of heat dissipation were calculated and discussed in details. Because of stability at high temperatures (up to 200 °C), preservation of photo-thermal efficiency at consecutive heating/cooling cycles and longtime durability, the synthesized CQDs nanofluid can be considered as a super-stable nanofluid. Additionally, the performance of the CQDs nanofluid in solar energy harvesting and its application for water heating in a continuous flow system was examined. All results confirm that the synthesized CQDs nanofluid has many advantages including low cost and simplicity of preparation procedure, no need to use dispersant, long-life stability, very high thermal-stability and high photo-thermal conversion efficiency. Therefore, CQDs nanofluid has high potential to be utilized for practical solar energy harvesting.

ce:def-description.ce:para: Prepared nanofluid (x is the microwave irradiation time for preparation of sample)
Specific heat of base fluid (J/kg K)
Difference between the initial and the instantaneous temperatures (K)
Difference of the CQDs nanofluid mass (kg)
Distance from the upper surface of working fluid (cm)
Top surface area of the light receiver (m2)
Heat flux of the incident solar (W/m2)

ce:section.ce:para: Global warming is generally believed to play a significant role in enhancing problems related to environmental concerns [1], which are more and more noticeable among the industrial countries. One efficient solution is development of renewable energy sources [1] as the fossil fuels emit greenhouse gases [2]. The fossil fuels have a key role in increasing global warming and environmental pollution [3]. Solar energy widely is used for heating purposes [4], as one of the best renewable and abundant energy sources [1,4–6] and potential to provide much of the growing energy demand [7]. It is free, easily available and accessible [1,5], inexhaustible [8] with green nature [2,9]. Generally, there are two approaches for harvesting of solar energy: (1) photo-electric conversion and (2) photo-thermal conversion [2,8]. Utilizing of sun radiation with photo-thermal conversion strategy is simpler than that of photoelectric conversion [3]. In photoelectric conversion, mainly the UV–Visible range of solar radiation is used to generate electricity; however, the whole range of wavelength in sun spectrum can be used for thermal energy production through the photo-thermal conversion [3]. The incident solar radiation can be stored as thermal energy in a device, called solar collector [3]. In 1975, Minardi and Chaung suggested the first concept of the direct absorption solar collectors (DASCs) [10]. DASC is a suitable strategy to enhance the solar radiation absorption and conversion [11] by decreasing the heat loss [12] and increasing thermal efficiency to minimize the limitations of conventional solar collectors [13]. It means that the solar radiation can be directly absorbed within fluid volume without intermediary heat transfer process [13]. Using an anti-reflection cover, which decreases the light reflection in designing the DASCs, is an efficient solution to minimize heat dissipation [11].

Nanofluids, as a new type of fluids, contain dispersed nanometer-sized particles [7], and show significant optical and thermal properties, such as high thermal conductivity [14], significant enhancement in the absorption of solar energy [7], and heat transport ability [15]. Nanofluids are used in DASCs because of their high efficiencies for solar thermal conversion [14]. An improvement in the absorption properties of liquids can be achieved by adding nanoparticles to increase the photo-thermal efficiency [16]. During solar irradiation, created micro-convection at the surrounding of suspended nanoparticles enhances the convective heat transfer [3]. A wide range of direct absorption of particles were used to enhance the photo-thermal performance, such as Au nanoparticles [14,17], multi-walled carbon nanotube (MWCNT) [12], modified graphene [18], Ag and Cu nanoparticles [14], carbon black (CB) [3], nanodots [19,20], and CuO nanoparticles [21]. In addition to being environmental-friendly, simple preparation procedure and low-cost primary materials, the stability of nanoparticles in the carrier fluid is an important factor for nanofluids since the precipitation of nanoparticles is their noticeable disadvantage. Among these nanomaterials, carbonaceous nanoparticles have gained researchers’ attention due to high light absorption [22] and superior thermal properties [23]. Boldoo et al. [23] investigated the functionalized and non-functionalized MWCNT nanofluids, and they found that the thermal conductivity enhancement, the photo-thermal conversion efficiency, and the optical and thermal properties of the functionalized MWCNT nanofluid are higher than that of the non-functionalized MWCNT nanofluid. For stabilization of non-functionalized and functionalized MWCNT nanofluids, they used surfactants and functionalization methods. For direct solar thermal collectors, Wang et al. [22] improved the stability of the carbon nanotube/water nanofluid using graphene oxide (without using any surfactants), which the
π - π
interactions between the graphene oxide and carbon nanotube have a key role in stabilization of the carbon nanotube in water. But long preparation time and having several components to produce nanofluids are not desirable. For direct absorption solar collectors, Chen et al. [24] found that the reduced-graphene oxide/water nanofluid, prepared under the UV irradiation time of 340 s or less, has high-thermal stability. They used Polyvinylpyrrolidone as surfactant for stabilization of nanofluids. Liu et al. [18] introduced a new way to enhance the stability of the graphene (GE)/ionic liquid (IL) nanofluids based on modification of GE according to the IL molecular structure but the process is long and needs several steps for modification. Adam et al. [25] investigated the effect of temperature on the stability and optical properties of SiO2-water nanofluids for hybrid photovoltaic/thermal applications, and reported that the absorbance of mentioned nanofluids decreased when the exposure temperature increased from 25 to 90 °C, and agglomeration more likely occurred for higher exposure temperatures. Chen et al. [7] found that the size of aggregated nanoparticle cluster has a significant effect on the light absorption of nanofluids.

There are some disadvantages for most of the nanofluids, used in photo-thermal conversion, such as utilization of surfactants for stabilization of dispersed nanoparticles [26], being multi-component [27,28], having multi- and long steps of synthesis process [9,29], high preparation cost [30], low thermal stability, etc.

Recently, carbonaceous nanofluids have been introduced for solar energy harvesting but some problems are found to use them. For example, reduced graphene oxide nanofluids were synthesized by time-consuming methods and using several components, including graphite powder, ammonia water, L-ascorbic acid, NaNO3, KMnO4, H2SO4, and H2O2 [2]. Ethylene glycol-based nanofluid, containing carbon black nanoparticles, was prepared using a procedure for >8 h, including several ultrasonication, filtration, precipitation, drying, and mixing [31]. Water-based MWCNT nanofluid was introduced but MWCNTs were synthesized using a high-cost chemical vapor deposition method, and they were stabled in the base fluid using TNWDIS as a surfactant [12]. Therefore, preparation of the high-stable, green, non-toxic, one-component and inexpensive carbonaceous nanofluids with fast methods seems to be a challenging necessity.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are one of the most important carbonaceous materials with physicochemical, optical and electronic properties. CQDs are used in different fields namely biosensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, optoelectronics, and photovoltaics [32]. The outstanding optical and electronic properties of CQDs including efficient light harvesting [32], tunable photoluminescence (PL) [32], large absorption coefficients and broad absorption spectra, extraordinary up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL) make them interesting materials for entire utilizing of the solar spectrum [32]. CQDs are obtained by various methods, including arc-discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, chemical oxidation, ultrasonic synthesis, microwave synthesis, thermal decomposition, hydrothermal treatment, template based routes, and plasma treatment [32]. Microwave heating is one of the most facile and efficient methods for this aim.

Polyethylene glycols (PEGs) are environmental-friendly polymers since they are non-toxic and highly biodegradable. Due to low toxicity, solubility in both water and organic solvents, and good biocompatibility, PEGs are widely used in food, pharmaceutical, biomedical, cosmetic, membrane, chemical industries, and as solvents and phase transfer catalyst in organic synthesis [33]. All mentioned benefits are the main reason for the selection of PEG as precursor in this work.

Herein, the stable CQDs dispersed in PEG 200 were synthesized using one-step microwave heating of single-substrate, PEG 200, for several minutes. Then, the obtained CQDs suspension, as green, stable and efficient nanofluid, was used for photo-thermal conversion. Additionally, its ability for water heating process via solar-thermal conversion was investigated.

The dispersed carbon nanoparticles were synthesized based on the previous reported methods using microwave heating [34–37]. Microwaves are part of the electromagnetic spectrum, ranging from 1 m to 1 mm, and can interact with polar materials [38]. The electric field component of microwaves is responsible for the dielectric heating of materials, which have dipole [38]. Under a high frequency electric field, the dipoles do not have sufficient time to respond to the oscillating field. As a result of this phase lag, they collide with each other when they attempt to follow the field, and the power is dissipated to generate heat in the material [38]. PEG has many dipoles, and therefore, the interaction between dipoles of PEG and electric field of microwaves leads to heat generation. So, the temperature rises intensively during microwave irradiation, causing carbonization of PEG. In the present work, the samples (Px, where x is the microwave irradiation time for preparation of sample) were prepared with microwave irradiation of 70 ml PEG 200 in a microwave oven (BITRON-BMW-25BGC) at 700 W for 4, 10, 20 min, respectively. The synthesis procedure is schematically shown on Fig. 1 in which the infrared thermographs of the obtained samples show their temperatures just after turning-off of the microwave irradiation. The reason behind the difference in temperatures of P4, P10, and P20 is related to duration of microwave heating for preparation. As it discussed above, PEG can absorb the microwave irradiation and generate heat, resulting increase in temperature. The microwave irradiation time for preparation of P4 is less than that for P10 and P20. Therefore, the heat generation and temperature increment of PEG are lower for P4 compared to P10 and P20. Turning the colorless PEG solvent to the dark solution (Fig. 1) indicates formation of dispersed carbon nanoparticles in PEG.

The synthesized carbon nanofluid was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Philips EM208s) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern Instruments Ltd, Zetasizer Ver. 7.11, serial number: MAL1153055). The morphology and particle size of the carbon particles in the synthesized nanofluid were analyzed using TEM. Transmission spectra of the synthesized nanofluid was recorded in the wavelength range of 190 to 900 nm using UV–Visible spectrophotometer (PG Instrument LTD model T80) and a cuvette with an optical-path length of 10 mm. Infrared thermal imaging camera (Hti HT-18) was used for monitoring the temperature of the synthesized carbon nanofluids. Fluorescence spectra of the sample were recorded using LS50 spectrofluorimeter (Perkin Elmer) equipped with a Xenon lamp and 1 cm quartz cell.

The UV–Visible spectra of the sample (diluted 500 times) at various time intervals was used to investigate the stability of the synthesized CQDs nanofluid. Moreover, the synthesized carbon nanofluid was exposed to a visible light source (50 W SMD lamp) for 100 min while its mass variation was recorded. Then, the diagram of (
Δ m /
m 0
) % vs.
t was plotted, which
Δ m
is the difference of the carbon nanofluid mass at time t with initial mass (
m 0
).

Fig. 2 shows the components of the photo-thermal conversion setup and the light source spectrum at 8 cm distance from the upper surface of the working fluid. The set up includes a cylindrical sample trough, made of glass (height: 5.4 cm; inner diameter: 3.6 cm; outer diameter: 4.6 cm) covered with epoxy resin and sponge to minimizes the heat loss. Three-temperature sensors were placed at different heights from fluid surface (0.2, 0.9, and 1.9 cm) connected to a data acquisition system (DAQ) and computer for recording the temperature variations during the experiment at an interval of 30 s. The visible light source (50 W SMD lamp) was located directly in various gaps (y) from the upper surface of the working fluid (y = 8, 11, and 13.5 cm). A light meter (SEKONIC Spectromaster C-7000) was used to record the light radiation spectrum between 380 and 780 nm at different distances (8, 11, and 13.5 cm). The light irradiance power at the mentioned distances was 868, 363, and 332 W/m2, respectively. Photo-thermal conversion was studied using deionized water, PEG 200, and the synthesized carbon nanofluid for 100 min of light irradiation. After turning on the light source, the temperature monitoring at different heights of the nanofluids was started and during the photo-thermal conversion experiments, the recorded temperature was plotted as a function of time. After 100 min of irradiation, the temperature of the fluids from different views (including surface temperature) was recorded with an infrared thermal imaging camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability of the synthesized CQDs nanofluid
ce:section.ce:para: Reusability is a crucial character of nanofluids to be utilized for photo-thermal conversion devices. The reusability of the synthesized CQDs nanofluid toward photo-thermal conversion in several cycles was studied. For this purpose, the synthesized carbon nanofluid was irradiated for six-consecutive cycles, which each cycle included 120 min of radiation (heating) and 240 min of cooling (light off). In each cycle, the temperature of the nanofluid was measured as a function of time.

Based on TEM images, the synthesized carbon nanofluid contained almost spherical carbon nanoparticles with various sizes (diameter < 20 nm), Fig. 3 a–c. As shown in the particle size distribution histogram (Fig. 3d), the diameter of about 90% of particles is <10 nm and the average diameter of carbon particles is 6.5 nm. This result clearly shows that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) formed during microwave irradiation of PEG 200.

It has been well demonstrated that there is a well-known relationship between particle size and band gap
(
E g
)
of quantum dots [39,40], confirming smaller particles have fewer atoms and wider band gaps [39]. Therefore, the emitted light of quantum dots shifts to the longer wavelength with increasing size. Fig. 5 a and b represent the fluorescence and the normalized fluorescence spectra of the synthesized CQDs nanofluid. With increasing excitation wavelength (
λ
ex
) from 330 to 480 nm, the fluorescence spectra shift to the higher wavelength while the intensity increases and then decreases. Appearance of the fluorescence spectra with different intensities indicates the presence of CQDs with different sizes. The variety in size distribution of the dispersed CQDs gives the ability to absorb light with various wavelengths, and consequently, release the absorbed energy as a mixture of heat and light. CQDs absorb the incident light and consequently the electrons, excited from valence band (V.B.) to conduction band (C.B.), which creates electron-hole pair. This process is called “generation” [41] (Fig. 5c). The difference between the incident photon energy and the electronic gap is wasted as heat [41]. The process by which excited electrons quickly release their excess energy until they reach the edge of the C.B. is known as “thermalization” [41], this process increases temperature in the nanofluids. Then, the relaxation of free electrons from the C.B. to a vacancy (hole) in the V.B. leads to the annihilation of an electron-hole pair (recombination) and release their excess energy as a photon. Now, these released photons are candidate to be absorbed by other CQDs, which have smaller band gaps (larger particles). As a result, CQDs nanofluid is able to absorb light with different wavelengths (UV and visible) and release them as heat (IR) (Fig. 5c) as confirmed by infrared thermograph camera. By considering two constrains, that the most intense fluorescence emission (
λ
ex
= 360 nm, Fig. 5a) belongs to the most abundant nanoparticles (size 3–5 nm, Fig. 3d) and also only quantum dots (size < 10 nm) are responsible for the fluorescence emission, the correlated plot of the particle size distribution and the relative fluorescence intensity was obtained and plotted in Fig. 6 . From this figure, it is possible to estimate the corresponding excitation and emission wavelength by each CQD particle. Thanks to the presence of CQDs with different sizes in the synthesized nanofluid, it is possible to absorb light with different wavelengths (for example solar light) and convert it to the heat (infrared radiation).

To investigate the stability of the synthesized nanofluid, UV–Visible spectra of the synthesized carbon nanofluid right after the synthesis and after 37 days were recorded, Fig. S1a (supporting information). The obtained results indicated that the synthesized carbon nanofluid is very stable because both spectrums match to each other. Based on Fig. S1b (supporting information), the sample is also stable under light irradiation.

The ability of the synthesized carbon nanofluid in photo-thermal conversion was investigated and compared with that of pure PEG 200 and deionized water. Fig. 7 a shows the variations of fluid temperature with irradiation time at three different heights from sample surface. For the synthesized carbon nanofluid, initially, the temperature rapidly increases, especially at the top layer, and then, the rate of the temperature increment becomes slower. It is obvious that the performance of all the synthesized carbon nanofluids in photo-thermal conversion is significantly better than that of pure PEG 200 and H2O. This is because of this fact that the synthesized carbon nanofluids have much lower transmittance compared to pure PEG 200 and H2O. In other words, most of the incident light is absorbed by the top layer of the carbon nanofluids but most of the incident light transmits through PEG 200 and H2O. Therefore, the temperature rise at the top layer (H1) of carbon nanofluid is higher than their bulk (H2 and H3). As the temperature increment of P10 was higher than P4, and also showed negligible difference with P20, therefore P10 was selected as the best sample for photo-thermal conversion. Fig. 7b represents the infrared thermographs of PEG 200 and the synthesized carbon nanofluids after light irradiation for 100 min. Based on the infrared thermography, the trend of temperature enhancement at different heights is H1 > H2 > H3 for all the carbon nanofluids, which is in agreement with the results in Fig. 7a. The infrared thermographs (Fig. 7b) show that the temperature of PEG is approximately homogeneous from the surface to the bottom of sample, indicating absorption of light by the whole of sample due to its transparency. While, the temperature profile for the carbon nanofluids is not homogeneous, indicating absorption of most of the light by the top layer. Also the IR images show that the surface temperature of P10 and P20 are higher than P4 and PEG, revealing good performance of P10 and P20 for photo-thermal conversion at the top layer of nanofluids.

Fig. 8 demonstrates six continuous heating/cooling cycles for the synthesized CQDs nanofluid. Similar trend was observed for all cycles, which means that at each height, the temperature reaches approximately the same value. Therefore, the carbon nanofluids are stable in heating/cooling cycles, and can be used for several times, which is very important for practical applications.

The effect of the carbon quantum dots in photo-thermal conversion performance was investigated by the stored energy ratio (SER). In fact, the SER value gives some information related to extra absorbed energy by the nanofluids because of the presence of the nanoparticles in comparison to the base fluid [4]. The SER equation is presented by Eq. (1) [4].
(1)
SER =
Q
nf
t
Q
bf
t
=
m
bf
c
bf
+
m
np
c
np
T
nf
t
-
T
nf
0
m
bf
c
bf
T
bf
t
-
T
bf
0
where,
Q
nf
t
,
Q

t
,
m
bf
,
m

,

,
c
np
,
T
nf

,
T

,
T
bf

,
T
bf
0
are heat stored by nanofluid at time t (s), heat stored by base fluid at time t, mass of base fluid (kg), mass of nanoparticles, specific heat of base fluid (J/kg K), specific heat of nanoparticles, temperature of nanofluid at time t, initial temperature of nanofluid, temperature of base fluid at time t and initial temperature of base fluid, respectively [4].

The Eq. (1) can be simplified to Eq. (2) since the mass and specific heat of nanoparticles are negligible compared to those of base fluid [4].
(2)
SER =
T
nf

T
nf
0

T
bf
0

The total absorbed energy (
E
total
)
by the nanofluid during the photo-thermal conversion was estimated using Eq. (3) [42]:
(3)
E
total
=
m
nf
c
nf
T
max
-
T
min
where,
m
nf
,
c
nf
,
T
max
, and
T
min
respectively represent the mass of carbon nanofluid, specific heat of nanofluid, and the maximum and minimum temperatures of carbon nanofluid. Since the carbon nanoparticles concentration is low,
c
nf
was equated to the same value of base fluid (PEG 200) [42]. Fig. 9 a shows that SER increases with time, goes through a maximum value, then decreases and reaches a stationary value. The results show that within the first 15 min the SER value at the top surface (H1) of the carbon nanofluid is about 2. This means that the storage energy at the top of carbon nanofluid is two times higher than base fluid (PEG 200), and at longer times, the SER value is 1.5. At deeper height (H2), the SER value is >1, and again indicating higher energy storage of carbon nanofluid in comparison to the PEG 200. However, at the deepest part (H3), the SER value is less than unity indicating the lower energy storage in the nanofluid because of its lower transparency. Furthermore, the total energy, absorbed for the carbon nanofluid at different heights and bulk, was calculated and compared with that of PEG (Table 1 ). The calculated absorbed energy and SER values indicated that photo-thermal conversion at the top layers of carbon nanofluid is high, and this region can be used for efficient conversion of light to heat.

The photo-thermal conversion efficiency ( η ) of the nanofluid and base fluid can be obtained from Eq. (4) [14,43] based on several assumptions [3]:
1)
The walls as well as bottom of the solar receiver are insulated and adiabatic.
2)
Heat loss due to convection or thermal re-radiation, which takes place at the upper surface of the nanofluid, are negligible.
3)
As the amount of nanoparticles, dispersed in fluid, are low, specific heat capacity and density of nanofluid are equal to those of the base fluid.
(4)
η =
m
nf
C
nf
GA
Δ T
Δ t
× 100
where,
Δ T
, G , A , and
Δ t
are the difference between the initial and the instantaneous temperatures, the heat flux of the incident solar (W/m2), the top surface area of the light receiver (m2), and the time exposed to the light radiation, respectively. Fig. 9b shows the photo-thermal conversion efficiency of the CQDs nanofluid at various heights (H1, H2, H3) and at bulk. The result reveals that the photo-thermal conversion efficiency of the sample reaches almost 100% at H1, which is higher than those of bulk (~37%) and bottom (~10%) since it absorbs most of the incident light at its surface because of its dark color. As shown in Fig. 9b, the conversion efficiency is about 100% at about 30 °C, because the heat loss to the environment is negligible.

When the nanofluid is exposed to the light irradiation, it absorbs the incident light and converts it into heat with a constant rate. Then, the nanofluid temperature quickly rises since the heat dissipation rate is very low as the temperature difference between nanofluid and environment is negligible. After that, the difference of temperature between nanofluid and environment becomes more and more remarkable, and therefore, the rate of temperature enhancement decreases. So, the heat dissipation role becomes significant. The mentioned process is known as the heating stage [44]. When temperature reaches its equilibrium value (
T
eq
), the heat generation rate is equal to the heat dissipation rate (the equilibrium stage) [44]. After shutting down the light radiation, the nanofluid cools down and then reaches the ambient temperature (
T
am
) (cooling stage) [44]. The rate constant of the heat dissipation ( B ) can be derived from the slope of linear plot of
L n
T
t
-
T
am
T
eq
-
T
am
versus t based on Eq. (5) [44].
(5)
L n
T
t
-
T
am
T
eq
-
T
am
= - B t
where,
T
t
is the temperature of nanofluid at time t.

The rate constant of heat dissipation was obtained for the CQDs nanofluid at three-different heights based on Eq. (5) and its linear plot, Fig. 10 . It is clear that the B value depends on the height from the surface of nanofluid. The constant rate of heat dissipation for H1 (
B
H 1
= 0.0134
min−1) is higher than that of H2 (
B
H 2
= 0.0128
min−1) and H3 (
B
H 3
= 0.0123
min−1). The ratio of B values (
B
H 1
:
B
H 2
:
B
H 3
= 1.10 : 1.05 : 1.00
) indicates that the rate of heat dissipation at top layer (H1) is only 10% higher than the bottom (H3). Moreover, in comparison with the obtained value of B for other systems [44,45], the B value in the present work is lower, indicating that the rate of heat dissipation during photo-thermal conversion is lower.

All of the obtained parameters (SER,
E
total
,
η
, and B ) reveal that the upper part of CQDs nanofluid is very efficient for photo-thermal conversion.

The key points of the synthesized CQDs nanofluid are simplicity of preparation, economical, single-precursor, one-pot synthesis, fast preparation, and environmental-friendly nature. On the other hand, there is no necessity for using surfactant (because the obtained CQDs nanofluid is naturally highly stable), salt, ultrasonic, expensive precursors, oxidation/reduction reagents, and various chemicals. Therefore, both PEG 200 (precursor) and the CQDs nanofluid (product) are green and cheap, so the product would be attractive for the large-scale applications. A comparison between the synthesized CQDs nanofluid and other nanofluids are presented in Table 2 .

The capability of the synthesized CQDs nanofluid for conversion of solar energy to heat was investigated. In a sunny day with sun-light intensity of 810 W/m2 ( ≅ 0.81 Sun) and at ambient temperature of 26 °C, the CQDs nanofluid was exposed to the sun-light irradiation and the temperature change was monitored as a function of time. The temperature of nanofluids increased by time and after one hour its surface temperature reached 65 °C, while by LED lamp irradiation, it reached 55 °C (Fig. 11 a). This means that the synthesized CQDs nanofluid is very efficient for solar-thermal conversion. In another experiment, the intensity of solar irradiation was increased to 10 Sun using a magnifying glass, and was irradiated to the CQDs nanofluid. The temperature rise of the sample at different positions was recorded as a function of time (Fig. 11b). The surface temperature increased rapidly, and after 10 min and 1 h, it reached 100 °C and 145 °C, respectively. These results confirm that the CQDs nanofluid is capable for conversion of intensified light to heat, effectively. One of the main advantages of the synthesized CQDs nanofluid is its thermal stability, since it has been prepared at about 300 °C. As shown in Fig. 11b, the temperature of the CQDs nanofluid can be increased to 145 °C using solar-thermal conversion without losing stability or performance.

As the CQDs nanofluid showed the high performance in solar-thermal conversion, a simple system was designed for continuous water heating, Fig. 12 . As shown in Fig. 12a, a round copper coil was immersed in the top layer of the CQDs nanofluid, and after exposure for 30 min to sun-light irradiation (0.84 Sun), the temperature of system reaches 63 °C, the optical and infrared images are presented in Fig. 12a and b, respectively. Then, the water with flow rate of 30 ml/min, was passed through the copper coil, and the entrance/exit water temperature was monitored at different time intervals (Fig. 13 a–c). The temperature of water increased from 32.5 °C to 38.5 °C and was constant during water flowing through copper coil. After 30 min, about one liter of warm water was collected. Therefore, by utilization of the CQDs nanofluid, professional design of a water flow system, and also concentrated solar irradiance by parabolic mirror, it is possible to convert the available and cost-free solar energy to thermal energy, especially for the aim of water heating in large scale.

Nanofluids are promising materials for improving of solar-thermal conversion efficiency. A single and green component (PEG 200), and one-step fast procedure was utilized to synthesize CQDs nanofluid. The synthesized nanofluid with average carbon particle size of 6.5 nm showed noticeable and longtime dispersion stability due to strong solvation of nanoparticles with PEG 200. The synthesized nanofluid showed very good photo-thermal conversion efficiency. The synthesized CQDs nanofluid absorbs most of the solar energy and transfers it to the thermal energy at the region close to the surface of the working fluid, confirmed by infrared thermographs, because CQDs have large absorption coefficients and broad absorption spectra [46]. Thanks to the wide range of the CQDs sizes, the light with different wavelengths can be absorbed and more heat generation can be occurred because of thermalization process. In the synthesized nanofluid, PEG serves as a solar absorber as well as a medium for heat transfer. Carbon quantum dots act as photon acceptor and local heater for conversion of solar energy to the thermal energy. In addition, micro-convection in the surrounding of suspended nanoparticles is created, leading to more and more convective heat transfer [3]. Therefore, the temperature of the fluid rapidly increases to 50 °C within 10 min and to 65 °C within one hour of solar irradiation. The CQDs nanofluid can be used for photo-thermal conversion and for many cycles without losing performance. The obtained results showed that the rate of heat dissipation at the surface of the CQDs nanofluid is only 10% higher than the bottom part. The results of the present study confirmed that the CQDs nanofluid can be used for warming of water in a continuous flow system using solar harvesting. In summary, the remarkable benefits of the synthesized CQDs nanofluid are: utilization of only one precursor, fast and simple synthesis method, no need to dispersant, low preparation cost, longtime dispersion stability, high thermal stability, high efficiency for photo-thermal conversion, stable performance at consecutive heating/cooling cycles, and capability for water heating using solar harvesting. The present work paves the way for application of thermally stable CQDs nanofluid for large-scale production of hot water using concentrated solar irradiance by parabolic mirror.
Fatemeh Seifikar: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Saeid Azizian: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge the financial support (grant number 98-146) of Bu-Ali Sina University, and technical support of S. Mahmoudi and M.R. Khodadadian.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.enconman.2020.113675.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
